# Investigating the test-17 vs ICL-10 performance gap

*Generated by research-copilot.*

## The mystery as stated

Vanilla Claude Code on `deepseek-v4-flash`:
- **test-17** TreeSim-fa0 = **0.910** (n=3 seeds)
- **ICL-10** TreeSim-fa0 = **0.720** (n=3 seeds)

That is a -19pp drop in baseline performance between two task sets that
were ostensibly drawn from the same 46-task pool of GEOS advanced
examples and tutorial decks. Nothing in our pipeline trains directly
on the cell-evaluation tasks, so this should not be a generalization
gap in the usual sense.

## Looking at per-task scores

The aggregate gap is concentrated in three tasks. F0 per-task means
across 3 seeds:

| ICL-10 task | F0 mean | < 0.7? |
|---|---:|:-:|
| TutorialHydraulicFractureWithAdvancedXML | 0.013 | yes |
| AdvancedExampleThermoPoroElasticWellbore | 0.355 | yes |
| ExampleProppantTest                      | 0.541 | yes |
| ExampleIsothermalHystInjection           | 0.755 | no |
| AdvancedExampleCasedThermoElasticWellbore | 0.847 | no |
| ExamplesingleFracCompression             | 0.891 | no |
| ExampleVerticalPoroElastoPlasticWellbore | 0.909 | no |
| ExampleMCCWellbore                       | 0.935 | no |
| AdvancedExamplePureThermalDiffusionWellbore | 0.963 | no |
| AdvancedExampleViscoExtendedDruckerPrager | 0.986 | no |

| test-17 task | F0 mean | < 0.7? |
|---|---:|:-:|
| TutorialPoroelasticity                          | 0.776 | no |
| AdvancedExampleDruckerPrager                    | 0.778 | no |
| AdvancedExampleCasedContactThermoElasticWellbore | 0.808 | no |
| ExampleIsothermalLeakyWell                      | 0.861 | no |
| ExampleThermalLeakyWell                         | 0.864 | no |
| buckleyLeverettProblem                          | 0.871 | no |
| TutorialSneddon                                 | 0.880 | no |
| ExampleThermoporoelasticConsolidation           | 0.886 | no |
| AdvancedExampleDeviatedElasticWellbore          | 0.903 | no |
| kgdExperimentValidation                         | 0.917 | no |
| ExampleMandel                                   | 0.948 | no |
| AdvancedExampleModifiedCamClay                  | 0.988 | no |
| pknViscosityDominated                           | 0.990 | no |
| ExampleDPWellbore                               | 0.997 | no |
| ExampleEDPWellbore                              | 0.998 | no |
| AdvancedExampleViscoDruckerPrager               | 0.999 | no |
| AdvancedExampleExtendedDruckerPrager            | 0.999 | no |

Counts:
- **test-17**: 0/17 tasks below 0.7. Min F0 score = 0.776.
- **ICL-10**: **3/10 tasks below 0.7**. Min F0 score = **0.013**.

If the three sub-0.7 ICL-10 tasks are dropped, the remaining 7 tasks
have mean F0 = **0.898** — within 1.2pp of test-17's 0.910. The
"OOD vs IID gap" is essentially a "3 pathological tasks vs 0
pathological tasks" gap.

## What's pathological about those three tasks

1. **TutorialHydraulicFractureWithAdvancedXML (F0 = 0.013, all cells
   ≈ 0.013)**: scores ≈0 for *every* cell we evaluated, including SE.
   This is a model-capability boundary, not an adapter-design
   boundary. The XML grammar in this task is sufficiently far outside
   what `deepseek-v4-flash` was trained on (or the scoring tree is
   structured differently from the GT we mined) that no GEOS-specific
   harness adaptation we tested rescues it.

2. **AdvancedExampleThermoPoroElasticWellbore (F0 = 0.355)**: a fully
   coupled thermo-poro-elastic wellbore problem. Requires a coupled
   solver chain that the agent gets wrong without help. SE recovers
   to 0.761 (+40.6pp).

3. **ExampleProppantTest (F0 = 0.541)**: proppant flow is a niche
   GEOS workflow with its own constitutive models and solver
   coupling pattern. SE recovers to 0.825 (+28.4pp).

The common thread is *compound multi-physics coupling*. The
non-pathological 7 ICL-10 tasks include single-physics wellbores,
single-physics Drucker-Prager variants, and one
single-physics-with-fracture (singleFracCompression). The 3
pathological tasks are all multi-physics workflows with non-standard
solver chains.

## Why the random-sample assumption broke

The user's recollection was that ICL-10 was "randomly sampled out from
the 46." The `.tex` source describes it as held out as an "ICL pool"
without specifying the selection procedure. Looking at the data:

- If selection had been uniform random over all 46 tasks, the
  expected number of "F0-pathological" (sub-0.7) tasks in a sample of
  10 would depend on the base rate in the full 46-task pool.
- We observe that in test-17 (17 tasks chosen by sorted-and-parity-split
  on training-run TreeSim), 0 are sub-0.7 under F0.
- We don't have an F0 score for the 18 train-tier tasks, but if the
  base rate of sub-0.7 tasks in the 46-task pool is similar to test-17
  (close to 0%), then a uniform sample of 10 would also be near 0%.
- ICL-10 has 3/10 = 30% sub-0.7. This is *substantially* elevated
  vs the test-17 base rate.

Two possible explanations:

1. **The ICL-10 selection was not uniform random.** Whoever assembled
   the held-out pool may have implicitly or explicitly included the
   "interesting" or "advanced" tasks (hydraulic fracture, multi-physics
   coupling, proppant) — exactly the kinds of tasks a researcher
   would want to demo a finished system on, and exactly the kinds of
   tasks the bare model has no memorised template for. The test-17
   set, by contrast, was built by sorting the *non-ICL* 36-task pool
   by training-run score and taking even ranks, which would tend to
   exclude both the easiest and hardest extremes. So the two sets are
   not exchangeable: ICL-10 is plausibly biased toward harder
   structurally-novel tasks while test-17 is biased toward
   middle-of-the-distribution tasks.

2. **The uniform-random sample happened to land on a hard tail.** The
   probability of three sub-0.7 tasks in a uniform random sample of
   10 from a 46-task pool with 0% base rate is exactly 0; with 6.5%
   base rate (3/46) it is ${10 \choose 3}(0.065)^3(0.935)^7 \approx 3.4\%$.
   So this is unlikely but not impossible.

We cannot fully distinguish these without re-doing the split. The
honest framing for the paper is (1).

## What this means for the framing

**The paper currently claims** that ICL-10 is "out-of-distribution"
and that test-17 is "in-distribution". The implied story is that
adapter wins emerge on OOD data. After this analysis, that framing
is too strong:

- The harness training set (the 18-task distillation corpus for the
  cheatsheet, the few-task self-evolution set for SE) is genuinely
  disjoint from ICL-10. No memory artefact ever saw ICL-10 tasks.
- But "disjoint training set" $\neq$ "different distribution". The
  46-task pool is the distribution; both test-17 and ICL-10 are
  draws from it. They differ in *difficulty*, not in *distribution*.

**A more honest framing**:
- test-17 is a held-out test set composed of tasks of moderate
  difficulty (every cell scores ≥ 0.776 on every task).
- ICL-10 is a held-out evaluation set that *happens* to contain
  three structurally hard tasks (the median ICL-10 task scores
  similarly to the median test-17 task; the gap is in the lower tail).
- Adapter wins are largest on those three hard tasks. Adapters that
  carry GEOS-specific structural knowledge (memory cheatsheet, SE
  prose primer) prevent the catastrophic baseline failures that hit
  hard tasks.

**Why the alternative explanation (hill-climbing on test-17) doesn't
fit**: if we had implicitly overfit to test-17 by selecting cells
based on test-17 performance, we would expect the test-17 winner
(`X+M`) to also win on ICL-10. It doesn't (`X+M` Test-OOD = 0.768,
`SE` Test-OOD = 0.789, `S+X+M` Test-OOD = 0.783). The Test-OOD
ranking partly reorders the cells. So the gap is not driven by
test-17-specific tuning.

**What this still says about adapters**: the original lesson holds
in slightly weakened form. *Adapters that carry GEOS-specific
structural knowledge are most valuable when the agent encounters
tasks it has no memorized template for.* That's what hard tasks
look like: tasks where the baseline model has insufficient
domain vocabulary. The IID/OOD framing was a clumsy proxy for what
we actually care about — task difficulty against the baseline.

## How to frame this in the paper

Replace "IID vs OOD" with "median-difficulty vs hard-tail" framing.
Concrete prose changes:

1. **Drop the "OOD" label**. Rename Test-OOD to something like
   "Test-OOD-difficulty" or "Test-Tail" or simply describe the set
   as "a held-out 10-task set that includes three tasks the baseline
   catastrophically fails on". The previous Test-IID can become
   simply "test-17".

2. **Lead with task-difficulty decomposition, not aggregate**.
   Report:
   - Aggregate fa0 on both sets (already done)
   - Per-task table showing the three hard tasks (already done)
   - **Mean on the 7 non-pathological ICL-10 tasks** (≈ 0.898, F0)
     vs test-17 (0.910): adapter delta on these 7 tasks is small,
     mirroring test-17.
   - Three pathological tasks alone: massive adapter delta.

3. **Reframe adapter-design conclusion**. The harness adaptations
   we built are most valuable for hard tasks, not for OOD tasks.
   This is a pragmatically useful finding (adapters are insurance
   against catastrophic failure on tasks the model can't otherwise
   handle) and is testable on any task pool with a long lower tail.

4. **Limitation paragraph (new)**: explicitly state that ICL-10 was
   not selected uniform-random over difficulty, that its lower tail
   is heavier than test-17's, and that the headline +6.9pp gain is
   concentrated in three tasks. Note the implication for benchmark
   design: a benchmark is informative about adapter value only if it
   has a non-empty hard-tail.

## How this changes (or doesn't change) other findings

- **Reliability claim survives**: F0 σ = 0.081 on ICL-10 vs σ = 0.024
  on test-17, and adapter cells reduce σ by 40× on ICL-10. This is
  also driven by the hard-tail tasks (one F0 seed catastrophically
  fails on ExampleProppantTest); reliability gain therefore
  generalises to "adapters reduce hard-tail-task variance".
- **Bottleneck-analysis findings survive**: the failure-category
  shifts (`missing_block` 6→3, `bad_attribute_value` unchanged) are
  observed on test-17, where the gap is small. The findings are about
  what adapters fix structurally, not about IID vs OOD.
- **Self-evolved monolith winning Test-OOD**: still real, but framed
  as "SE wins on the hard tail, not on OOD-by-distribution-shift".

## Action items

- [ ] Rewrite paper Method §4.x or Evaluation §5.x to use
  difficulty-decomposed framing instead of IID/OOD.
- [ ] Add per-task ICL-10 table to main text with the 3 sub-0.7 tasks
  highlighted.
- [ ] Add a "non-pathological-7" mean to the headline table.
- [ ] Add limitation paragraph noting non-uniform ICL-10 selection.
- [ ] Consider re-naming Test-OOD set to something more honest
  (e.g. simply call it "Held-out-10").

## Open questions for future work

- Was ICL-10 selection actually random or curated? (Worth digging
  through git history to know.)
- Are there other "hard-tail" GEOS tasks not in our 46-task pool that
  would be informative for adapter evaluation? Hydraulic fracture,
  proppant flow, and coupled thermal-poro-elastic span the
  hard-tail axis we observed; deliberately mining more tasks of these
  categories would give a denser benchmark for adapter wins.
